cross_connections.display_label: Aunt was the 'young lady in New Haven' Edwards described.
Great-granddaughter of the Yale founder Pierpont.
Brother-in-law twice over: married her sister Hannah.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Brockett brothers married two Pierpont sisters
narrative_blocks.body: She married Richard Brockett in March 1756; the year before, her sister Hannah had married Richard's brother Abel. Their children were double first cousins on both the Brockett and Pierpont sides.

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of Edwards's 'young lady in New Haven'
narrative_blocks.body: Her aunt Sarah Pierpont Edwards was the 'young lady in New Haven' Jonathan Edwards famously described in 1722, the year before he proposed marriage to her. Mary was Edwards's niece-by-marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-granddaughter of Yale's founder
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandfather Rev. James Pierpont was co-founder of Yale College in 1701 and pastor of First Church New Haven for nearly thirty years. The Brockett genealogy notes she was 'a grand-daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker' through this same Pierpont line.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-four in North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: She died at North Haven, Connecticut on June 21, 1773 at age 34. She was buried at Montowese Cemetery; her husband Richard remarried Jemima Jacobs in 1790 and lived to age 85.

narrative_blocks.body: Her aunt Sarah Pierpont Edwards was the 'young lady in New Haven' Jonathan Edwards famously described in 1722, the year before he proposed marriage to her. Mary was Edwards's niece-by-marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-granddaughter of Yale's founder
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandfather Rev. James Pierpont was co-founder of Yale College in 1701 and pastor of First Church New Haven for nearly thirty years. The Brockett genealogy notes she was 'a grand-daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker' through this same Pierpont line.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-four in North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: She died at North Haven, Connecticut on June 21, 1773 at age 34. She was buried at Montowese Cemetery; her husband Richard remarried Jemima Jacobs in 1790 and lived to age 85.

units: New Haven Militia and Alarm List under Col. Russell
military_service.source_citation: Sheldon B. Thorpe, 'North Haven Annals' — Col. Russell's 1779 order to officers of the Alarm List and Militia of New Haven endorsed names of Richard Brockett, Ebenezer Brockett, and Isaiah Brockett
military_service.role: Officer on the New Haven Alarm List — the call-up roster for civilian-soldiers expected to respond rapidly to British raids along the Connecticut coast. Three Brockett brothers appeared together on Col. Russell's 1779 endorsement.

narrative_blocks.header: Dropped twenty shillings in fire, Assembly reimbursed him
narrative_blocks.body: Per Connecticut Colonial Records Volume 10, in 1776 Richard Brockett 'dropped a 20-shilling bill in the fire, and on proving that it was accidental, the General Assembly ordered the amount to be made good to him.' The case is one of the few documented Connecticut General Assembly currency-replacement decisions of the Revolutionary era — Continental and state paper currency being fragile, increasingly distrusted, and worth replacing only on solid evidence of innocent loss. The incident captures both the era's currency anxiety and Brockett's reputation: 'his word was as good as his bond.'

narrative_blocks.header: 1779 Alarm List with brothers Ebenezer and Isaiah
narrative_blocks.body: Per Sheldon B. Thorpe's 'North Haven Annals,' in 1779 Colonel Russell issued an order to the officers of the Alarm List and Militia of New Haven — on its back were endorsed the names of Richard Brockett, Ebenezer Brockett, and Isaiah Brockett, three Brockett brothers serving the New Haven militia in the war's middle years. The Alarm List was the call-up roster for civilian-soldiers expected to respond rapidly to British raids along the Connecticut coast. The three-brother endorsement appears in the manuscript record exactly that way.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Brockett brothers married two Pierpont cousins
narrative_blocks.body: Richard Brockett married Mary Pierpont on March 13, 1756 — her grandfather Rev. James Pierpont had founded Yale College and pastored First Church New Haven for thirty years, and her grandmother Mary Hooker Pierpont was the granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker. Richard's brother Abel Brockett married Mary's cousin Hannah Pierpont  — two Brockett brothers married two Pierpont cousins. Mary died at only thirty-four in 1773, but seven children survived: Joseph, Mary, Giles, Lydia, Richard Jr., and two ill-fated infant Jesses.

death.notes: Died on his 63rd birthday — born and died on the same calendar date Jan 17.

marriages.notes: DATE DISCREPANCY: Tree previously had 1782; Brockett Genealogy 1905 (primary family source) gives May 8, 1772. If 1772 correct, Joseph was 15 yrs 3 mos and Rebecca was 15 yrs 5 mos at marriage — extremely young but not impossible in colonial Connecticut. Counter-evidence for 1782: first child Roxanna born Nov 27, 1782 — if married 1772, 10-year gap before first surviving child is unusual but possible. Brockett family book treated as authoritative.

military_service.source_citation: Record of Conn. Men in the Revolution, State Library at Hartford, Manuscript Vol. pp. 547-548
military_service.role: Active Revolutionary War service in Sabin's regiment per the official Connecticut Men in the Revolution record; later rose to Captain in the Connecticut state militia.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on his sixty-third birthday, January 17
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Brockett was born January 17, 1757 in North Haven Connecticut and died January 17, 1820 in North Haven — exactly sixty-three years to the day. The same calendar date marked his entry into and departure from life, a striking biographical coincidence noted in family records and confirmed by his Montowese Cemetery burial. He was buried in the same churchyard where his Congregational parish under Rev. Benjamin Trumbull DD had centered the family's spiritual life.

narrative_blocks.header: Served in Sabin's Connecticut regiment, Revolutionary War
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Brockett's service in the American Revolution is documented in the 'Record of Connecticut Men in the Revolution' at the Connecticut State Library, where his name appears in Lieutenant Colonel Sabin's regiment. After the war he rose to Captain in the Connecticut state militia and was active in town and state affairs. He represented North Haven in the Connecticut General Assembly continuously from 1799 to 1806.

narrative_blocks.header: Through mother Mary Pierpont, Hooker descent confirmed
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Brockett's mother was Mary Pierpont — through whom this branch's Hooker descent runs. She was 'a grand-daughter of Rev. James Pierpont, the successor of Rev. John Davenport as pastor of the First Church in New Haven, and one of the founders of Yale College.' James Pierpont's wife was Mary Hooker, granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker — making Joseph a third-great-grandson of Thomas Hooker on his mother's side.

military_service.role: Connecticut militia officer; Lieutenant 1812 (war year) and Captain 1815 (postwar promotion).

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized with brother Pierpont in 1810
narrative_blocks.body: In 1810 Baptist preacher Rev. Joshua Bradley came to North Haven and drew crowds — among those converted were Jesse Brockett and his younger brother Pierpont, both baptized together and forming the nucleus of the new North Haven Baptist Church. Pierpont went on to forty-one years in the Baptist ministry; Jesse remained a layman but rose to deacon of the same North Haven church. The shared conversion shaped the next generation: Pierpont's son Edward Judson became chairman of the American Baptist Home Mission Society.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 lieutenant, postwar Connecticut militia captain
narrative_blocks.body: Jesse Brockett served as Lieutenant in 1812 at the outbreak of the War of 1812, then was promoted to Captain in 1815 in the postwar reorganization of the Connecticut militia. Like his younger brother Pierpont — who also served in the War of 1812 under Commander Abraham Hubbard at Groton and New London — Jesse answered the wartime call along Connecticut's vulnerable Long Island Sound coastline.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut General Assembly across two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Jesse Brockett served in the Connecticut General Assembly in 1829 and 1830, then returned a decade later for the 1839 session. He had also served for many years as Justice of the Peace in North Haven. The political career layered on his deacon role at the Baptist Church and his militia captaincy — a complete profile of the local-leadership Connecticut churchman-farmer of the early Republic.

naming_inspiration.description: First name 'Pierpont' honors paternal grandmother Mary Pierpont (H00371, 1738-1773) — through whom the Hooker descent runs (her grandfather Rev. James Pierpont married Mary Hooker, granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker).

military_service.role: Loyal-citizen enlistee at start of war; first made ensign, then promoted to lieutenant; served at Groton and New London to defend the Connecticut coast.

narrative_blocks.header: 41-year ministry, 6,300+ sermons, nine pastorates
narrative_blocks.body: Pierpont Brockett's active ministry spanned forty-one years and nine successive Baptist pastorates from his 1819 Canton Connecticut ordination through his 1862 retirement at Wethersfield. During those years he preached more than 6,300 sermons aside from addresses and lectures. His Connecticut and Massachusetts circuit moved him through Canton, Clinton, Essex, Agawam West Springfield, North Stonington, Saybrook, Lyme, Colchester, and finally Wethersfield 'in order to be near his children.'

narrative_blocks.header: 1810 Baptist conversion defied his Congregational mentor
narrative_blocks.body: Studious as a boy, Pierpont had been the 'favorite' of Rev. Benjamin Trumbull DD — historian of Connecticut and Congregational pastor at North Haven, who loaned him books and shaped his mental development. When Baptist evangelist Rev. Joshua Bradley came through North Haven in 1810, Pierpont and his brother Jesse were converted and baptized, forming the nucleus of a new Baptist church. Trumbull was grieved that his 'favorite boy' had left the Congregational fold and at first 'rebuked him strongly,' but finding he could not silence the young convert, ceased his opposition.

narrative_blocks.header: Ensign then lieutenant, Groton and New London
narrative_blocks.body: During the War of 1812 Pierpont enlisted as 'a loyal citizen called to take up arms for the defense of his country' under Commander Abraham Hubbard. He was first made ensign and then promoted to lieutenant, with service at Groton and New London on the Connecticut coast. After the war he undertook theological study at Danvers Massachusetts under Rev. Jeremiah Chaplin DD — whose classmates included two of the first American missionaries sent to Burma after Adoniram Judson.

narrative_blocks.header: Three years as Connecticut State Prison chaplain
narrative_blocks.body: From 1854 through 1857 — during a Wethersfield pastorate that spanned 1849 to 1862 — Pierpont's duties as Chaplain of the Connecticut State Prison required his whole time and he stepped away from regular pastoral service. The state prison chaplaincy combined religious counsel with the era's emerging penal-reform conversation. He returned to the Wethersfield pulpit in 1857 and continued there until his 1862 retirement.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into Great Barrington Ives family, 1779
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Brockett married James Ives of Great Barrington Massachusetts on June 16, 1779 — at the height of the American Revolution. She was twenty when she married into one of the leading Berkshire County families of western Massachusetts; her husband's town Great Barrington had been founded as the southern boundary of the colonial Berkshire frontier in 1761. Her brother Joseph Brockett would marry Rebecca Tuttle thirteen years before, while Mary's parents Richard and Mary Pierpont Brockett were still alive — Mary's mother died in 1773.

military_service.role: Enlisted as a youth in 1778, served three years through the war's later phase. Listed on the federal Pension List in 1832.

narrative_blocks.header: Three-year teen enlistment, 1832 federal pensioner
narrative_blocks.body: Giles Brockett enlisted at seventeen in 1778 in Colonel Mead's Connecticut Troop and served three full years through the Revolutionary War's later phase. His name appears on the federal Pension List in 1832 — the act that recognized aging Revolutionary veterans. After the war he tried sailing, making one or two voyages to the West Indies, then returned to North Haven to take up farming, where he lived to age eighty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Sailor first, then deacon and Connecticut legislator
narrative_blocks.body: After his short West Indies sailing career, Giles returned to farming and married Sarah Smith in 1785. In 1803 the family removed to Waterbury Connecticut, where Giles became deacon of the First Congregational Church. He served as deputy to the Connecticut General Court in 1804 and as representative in the Connecticut Legislature in 1809, plus active membership in the Masonic Lodge — a complete local-leadership Yankee farmer profile.

narrative_blocks.header: Six children at East Farms then Dublin Street
narrative_blocks.body: Giles and Sarah's six children included Polly, Sarah, Patty, Harriet (who married Lieutenant Colonel Samuel Peck), Roswell (who died unmarried in Greenville Michigan in 1853), and Lydia (who married Smith Miller). The Waterbury farm was first at East Farms and then on what later became Dublin Street. Giles and Sarah were remembered as 'persons of a happy temperament, lovely, generous, and beloved by their family.'

cross_connections.display_label: Lydia Brockett Blakeslee married Philemon Blakeslee (b.1760). Philemon's sister was.
Lydia Brockett Blakeslee married Philemon Blakeslee (b.1760). Philemon's sister was.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Philemon Blakeslee 1787, nine documented children
narrative_blocks.body: Lydia Brockett married Philemon Blakeslee on February 22, 1787 at age twenty-three. The couple had nine documented children — most without birth dates surviving, but including a daughter who was twin to her son Philemon and died young, and another daughter Lydia who died at age thirty. The most striking generational pattern: daughter Mary Blakeslee later married a cousin Pearly Blakeslee, keeping the Blakeslee surname in two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Tirzah baptized at St. John's Episcopal, 1792
narrative_blocks.body: Richard Brockett Jr. was born January 9, 1768 and died in 1832 at age sixty-four. His only documented child Tirzah Brockett was born in North Haven in 1792 and baptized that same year at St. John's Episcopal Church — a different church than his parents' Congregational membership at New Haven First Church. The shift to Episcopal practice in Tirzah's baptism marks one of the early-Republic Brockett-family denominational pivots, an era when Connecticut Episcopal congregations were growing from their post-Revolutionary 'Loyalist' status into a recognized mainstream denomination.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived a single day, January 1770
narrative_blocks.body: The first Jesse Brockett was born January 16, 1770 in North Haven and died the very next day — a one-day infant life, the briefest documented in the entire Richard-Mary Brockett seven-child sibling sequence. The name 'Jesse' was attempted a second time two years later when another son was born on February 10, 1772, but that second Jesse lived only thirteen days. Two named-Jesse infant deaths in three years marked the family's mid-1770s grief.

narrative_blocks.header: Second Jesse, died age thirteen days, 1772
narrative_blocks.body: After the first Jesse Brockett died the day after his January 1770 birth, Richard and Mary Pierpont Brockett named their next son Jesse as well — this second Jesse was born February 10, 1772 and lived only thirteen days, dying February 23. The repeated-name pattern was common in colonial families honoring a deceased child's intended name, but the second Jesse's also-early death meant the name passed entirely from this sibling line. The next surviving Jesse Brockett in the family — Jesse 1785-1850, son of Joseph and grandson of Richard — would carry the name forward.

cross_connections.display_label: Married the sister of his own sister's husband, both Pierpont-Russell siblings.

bio.bio_blurb: North Haven farmer with twelve children

military_service.notes: FindAGrave: Lieut Joseph Pierpont; North Haven farmer.

narrative_blocks.header: Double sibling marriage joined Pierponts and Russells
narrative_blocks.body: He married Hannah Russell around 1725 while his sister Mary married Hannah's brother William the same year. Two siblings on each side, married into a single family in one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His marriage joined two Yale-founding families
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. James Pierpont was a Yale founding trustee in 1701. His wife's father Rev. Noadiah Russell was another.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children at his North Haven farm
narrative_blocks.body: He farmed land about ten miles northeast of New Haven through the 1730s and 1740s. Several of his twelve children died in infancy, including the first James in 1727.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — Yale founding trustee 1701

cross_connections.display_label: East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
East Guilford ordination, November 25, 1706
Both founding trustees of Yale 1701; Pierson was first rector.
Pierpont built the institution Davenport had envisioned.
Descendant co-founded Skull and Bones at his Yale 130 years later.
coined the Ivy League name for colleges like Yale
his brother-in-law, a fellow Yale founder married to a Hooker sister

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Roxbury Latin School before Harvard.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait 1711 by Pierpont Limner; Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten ministers, forty books, one college
narrative_blocks.body: In 1700 Pierpont gathered ten ministers at Branford, each laying down volumes with the words “I give these books for the founding of a College in this Colony” — tradition credits James with six of the original forty. The charter he secured the next year became Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Two elms planted at the minister’s door
narrative_blocks.body: A poor farmer with nothing else to give carried two young elms on his shoulder and planted them before the new parsonage in 1686. Under their shade Jonathan Edwards courted Sarah Pierpont forty years on, and Whitefield preached at sixty. When the last elm finally came down, its sound trunk was sawed into blocks and scattered among the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brides in seven years
narrative_blocks.body: James buried two young wives in five years — Abigail, dead of a cold caught attending church in her wedding garments per the custom of the day, and Sarah, lost after childbirth. In 1698 he married Mary Hooker of Farmington, who bore eight children and outlived him by twenty-six years.

narrative_blocks.header: He drafted Connecticut’s church constitution
narrative_blocks.body: A leader of the 1708 Saybrook Synod, Pierpont is said to have drawn up the Articles of the Saybrook Platform — the system of discipline and fellowship that governed Connecticut’s churches for more than a century.

narrative_blocks.header: 1706 ordination — five ministers, all Hooker-tree
narrative_blocks.body: On November 25, 1706 he was among five ministers who ordained Rev. John Hart at East Guilford — alongside Buckingham of Saybrook, Noadiah Russell of Middletown, Samuel Russell of Branford, and Thomas Ruggles of Guilford. All five names run through the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: The household that seeded three centuries
narrative_blocks.body: The New Haven parsonage became one of America’s great seedbeds: daughter Sarah married Jonathan Edwards, and the lines that followed carried two Timothy Dwights and Theodore Dwight Woolsey to Yale’s presidency, a vice president named Burr — and, further on, a financier named John Pierpont Morgan.

education.notes: Oldest boys’ school in the colonies, founded 1645.

career.notes: Ordained July 2 1685, ending an eleven-year pulpit vacancy; served until death.
The school became Yale College in 1718.

cross_connections.display_label: fathered the New Haven minister she married in 1698
was mother to the New Haven minister she married in 1698
her granddaughter, last of the line the heirloom apron passed through

notable.notable_blurb: Wife of Yale co-founder Rev. James Pierpont

artworks.artwork_blurb: Companion portrait to husband Rev. James Pierpont, 1711; Yale Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children in the house under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: Mary ran the New Haven parsonage through sixteen years of marriage and eight children, then presided over it as a widow for twenty-six more. The congregation had raised the house by voluntary subscription, and it stood for over a century.

narrative_blocks.header: Married James Pierpont the year her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father, the Farmington minister Samuel Hooker, died in November 1697; she married James Pierpont of New Haven the following year. The match carried her from one parsonage to the center of Connecticut’s emerging intellectual capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter’s courtship under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: It was Mary’s daughter Sarah whom Jonathan Edwards courted beneath the parsonage elms in 1726 — a match made in her widowed household that joined the Pierpont and Edwards lines and led on to college presidents and a vice president.

narrative_blocks.header: A son lost crossing the Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Samuel — Yale 1718, newly ordained at Lyme — drowned in March 1723 crossing the Connecticut River in a canoe with an Indian guide. His body came ashore on Fishers Island six weeks later and was buried where it was found.

narrative_blocks.header: The family that named an artist
narrative_blocks.body: The unidentified colonial painter who took her portrait around 1711 is known to art history only as the Pierpont Limner — christened for this family, whose likenesses anchor his surviving work. Her husband’s companion portrait hangs at the Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: A silk apron may trace to her line
narrative_blocks.body: A rare 1730s embroidered silk apron at the Connecticut Historical Society descends through three generations of Marys beginning with her. Though scholars doubt she stitched it herself, her advantageous marriage made such a luxury possible for the daughters who followed.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mrs. James Pierpont (née Mary Hooker), 1711. Wikipedia Commons.
